tweets.text:
Must read from @NikkeiAsia 

"Chinese leader Xi Jinping turned 73 on Monday, and this week's China Up Close explores whether he is thinking about grooming a successor. It emerged this month that Cai Qi, China's security czar and Xi's closest aide, has become the president of the Party School of the Communist Party's Central Committee, which educates young elites.

While attention is focused on a reshuffle of China's top leadership team at the party's national congress in 2027, the appointment of 70-year-old Cai to the key post has sparked speculation. Political pundits point out that with the appointment, Xi sent a signal he will reject potential pressure for generational change.

When China held a military parade last September in Beijing, Xi and Russian President Vladimir Putin were caught on a hot mic chatting about human longevity. Xi reportedly said that these days 70-year-olds are still young and mentioned the possibility "of living to 150." Their chat might help us understand Xi's thinking about passing the torch to the next generation."

tweets.text:
How to get your first 500 users as a solo founder in 2026 (even if you suck at marketing) ↓

1. Reach out to friends, coworkers, classmates, gaming buddies, etc. Ask for feedback. If the product is useful, some of them will naturally become users.

2. Post on X, Instagram, TikTok, Reddit, LinkedIn, or wherever your audience is. Show what you’re building, what’s broken, and what you’re improving.

3. Find niche subreddits, X communities, Discord groups, etc. Join the conversation, be useful, post publicly, and DM people when your product is relevant.

4. Put it on Product Hunt, Hacker News, BetaList, Peerlist, AppSumo, Indie Hackers, Dailypings, and niche directories.

5. Offer free trials, VIP access, early-user perks, or exclusive tools. Once someone signs up and likes it, ask them for one referral.

Your first 500 users usually come from boring manual outreach.

This is the period to get all the early confirmations and validation before you start scaling.

it sits below ~$7B of debt

in a liquidation of the company (which admittedly isn’t very likely imo), he’d have to pay $7B to covert holders first, in cash. he can’t pay them BTC in kind and mark it to market. he’d have to sell as much as he needs to pay $7B greenback united states dollarinos

now remember a scenario where the company winds down doesn’t happen with btc at $60k, this process would only begin if we’re at something like $30k and the sky is falling. so he begins the process with his “btc treasury” valued at less than $30B marked to market, but then he needs to sell $7B to cover the debt, how much further does that suppress the price? by that point his entire remaining treasury would probably be valued at under $10B (STRC outstanding) and that’s before he starts selling it

now look like i said i don’t think this happens. but if you want to value this paper fundamentally based on liquidation preference then that’s the analysis

tweets.text:
Monument Bank recently announced plans to tokenize £250m in retail deposits on Midnight.

@MidnightNtwrk's mainnet has been live since March 2026, and a major regulated bank has already committed to migrating customer savings to it.

This may be bigger for privacy adoption than you think ↓

1️⃣ The privacy problem

Privacy in crypto has existed for years, but it was always built around total anonymity.

That's fine for some use cases, but it's not what institutions need.

What they need is selective privacy.

A bank needs to keep customer transactions private from the public, but it also has to show regulators a clean audit trail.

A business needs to protect commercial data but still prove compliance when required.

Most privacy chains leave compliance to individual apps. That makes institutional adoption harder.

2️⃣ The selective privacy model

@MidnightNtwrk is approaching this differently.

Instead of hiding everything by default, Midnight lets you choose what stays private and what can be disclosed, all at the contract level.

The token-resource model is built around that same separation:

→ $NIGHT is public by design, so your holdings are visible and verifiable
→ Holding $NIGHT generates DUST, a private resource that pays for transaction execution without exposing activity

Compliance and privacy aren't competing. They're baked in together.

3️⃣ The institutional signal

Monument, a major UK-regulated bank, just committed £250m of customer deposits to Midnight.

These aren't institutional pilots or closed tests. These are retail customer savings, fully backed and covered by the same deposit protections as any other UK bank account.

Monument isn't a small player either. They have around $9.5B in deposits, with more than 100,000 customers.

For me, it's a clear signal that institutions need the selective privacy model.

The institutional side is only half of the story.

Midnight's bigger bet is that privacy should be invisible. Users shouldn't have to think about keys, wallets, or what's exposed onchain. They should be able to tap, authenticate, and have their sensitive data protected automatically.

That's a long way from where crypto sits today, as wallets are too complex, and transactions are usually fully transparent.

Midnight tackles all of these at the protocol level.

The same mechanism that works for regulated banks also lets everyday users prove who they are without handing over personal data or pay for something without exposing their balance.

I'd almost given up on the idea of regulated banks showing up on a privacy chain.

One of the more overlooked stories in the #uranium sector right now may be Noble Plains Uranium $NOBL $NOBL.V.

At a market cap of roughly US$6M, the company already controls a 6.36 Mlb NI 43-101 uranium resource at Duck Creek in Wyoming. What's particularly interesting is that the current resource was defined after drilling only about half of the known 5.25-mile mineralized trend.

In other words, the resource exists today, but the exploration story is far from over.

The recently announced LOI with UR-Energy adds another layer to the investment case:

• Access to nearby ISR infrastructure
• Potential for a more streamlined development pathway
• Reduced capital intensity versus standalone development
• Strategic alignment with an established Wyoming uranium producer

As uranium fundamentals continue to strengthen and long-term contract prices trend higher, the market may begin placing greater value on companies with existing resources, district-scale upside, and realistic development pathways.
For a company valued at only a few million dollars, Noble Plains offers exposure to all three.

Disclosure: Paid partnership with Noble Plains Uranium. Not financial advice. Always do your own due diligence.

tweets.text:
BitMine is paying 9.5% to hold an asset that yields 3%.

And every time $ETH dumps, Tom Lee just opens the BitMine wallet and buys more $ETH.

is BitMine running a smart ETH treasury strategy or quietly becoming the biggest ETH bagholder in the market?

I think the answer's somewhere in the middle.

BitMine now holds around 5.6M ETH, worth roughly $9B+, or about 4.66% of total ETH supply.

Their target is what they call the “Alchemy of 5%.”

• Own ~5% of all ETH supply.
• Stake most of it.
• Earn ETH yield on that stack.
• Use that yield to keep compounding.

It’s kinda like MicroStrategy stacking BTC, with one difference. $ETH is not just sitting there.

BitMine already has around 4.7M ETH staked, which can generate roughly $220M to $270M per year at current staking yields.

Tom Lee’s trying to build a public-market ETH compounding machine.

And this is where MAVAN comes in. It's BitMine's own staking and validator network.

So after they conclude the aggressive buying phase, the plan is to own enough ETH, stake it through their own infra, earn the rewards, and eventually turn that infra into something institutions can plug into too.

Now here's the part I keep coming back to though. The funding side.

BitMine raised money through preferred shares, which basically means investors give them capital and BitMine has to pay them a fixed dividend.

That dividend is 9.5%, while ETH staking yield is closer to 3%. They're paying almost three times what the asset earns.

That gap is fine when ETH is stable, the stock trades well, and investors are still willing to fund the strategy.

But if ETH keeps falling and BitMine’s stock stops trading above the value of its ETH holdings, raising the next round gets harder and a lot more expensive.

If that happens it can get ugly fast.

So the thing I'm watching isn't how much ETH BitMine owns.
It's what happens after they hit 5%.

Is BitMine becoming the MicroStrategy of $ETH with staking yield baked in? Or is one of ETH's biggest buyers almost done buying?

.@hwwonx has been a steadfast contributor to the Ethereum ecosystem for a decade. I still remember her early days in the Ethereum research community, first outside the Foundation and then inside it, and the thought and care she put into making Ethereum research and consensus work more organized and legible. At the same time, she put a lot of work into building an excellent Ethereum community in Taipei, with people and events that were among my favorites.

Last year she, along with @tkstanczak, voluntarily took on the burden of what is perhaps the most challenging position in the Ethereum Foundation, at one of the most challenging times for Ethereum - and realistically, a challenging time for all of humanity. She handled the task skillfully and gracefully, and has constantly strived to find and insist on outcomes that are right both for the Ethereum protocol and for the human beings that build and maintain it.

I look forward to her next adventures.

I have written in my STRC article that there were roughly 4 main variables for demand in STRC:

- Credit risk (depends on MSTR leverage ratio and BTC price)
- STRC yield / dividend rate
- Awareness around the instrument
- Confidence

Right now we're seeing the confidence lever getting hurt, and it's the variable that has the quickest impact on STRC price.

I'm not of the opinion that 'once confidence is lost, it's over', because expecting STRC not to trade below $99 was stupid from the beginning, and it's definitely not necessary to hold this view to buy it.

tweets.text:
The 2-10 Yr curve is rolling over, telling us that Warsh is really trying to tame the longer-term inflation expectations.  

We'll see what credit spreads have to say here.  If they widen, then he is taking too much liquidity out of the system and could cause a liquidity shock.

If they don't widen, he will be threading the needle- hammering inflation without deflating the growth (this will be really hard, but possible).

I wonder if the real policy he can't say publicly is trying to get longer duration treasury yields lower so that Bessent can term out the Treasury debt and not issue 85% on the front end like a 3rd world country.

He may also implicitly be trying to get mortgage costs lower so we have a generational turnover in boomer housing- this will be really hard if he ends up pricking the growth bubble.
